
::: {.paper-table}
**Table 28. C.1 Social priming — your environment reshapes you continuously**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Elderly-trait priming slows walking | d =0.82–1.08 | Merely reading words like “Florida” or “bingo” makes you physically slower for the next ~30 seconds | Bargh, Chen & Burrows [@bargh1996] |
| Warm cup → warm personality judgment | r =0.28–0.31 | Briefly holding a hot vs. cold beverage substantially changes how trustworthy you find the next stranger you meet | Williams & Bargh [@williams2008] |
| Money priming → selfish behavior | d =0.34–0.75 | Seeing a picture of cash makes you measurably less helpful for the rest of the session | Vohs, Mead & Goode [@vohs2006] |
| Professor priming → better trivia performance | 13% accuracy gain | Thinking about a professor before a quiz makes you score as if you had studied more | Dijksterhuis & van Knippenberg 1998 |
| Macbeth effect (moral cleansing) | d =0.6–0.9 | Recalling an unethical act makes you prefer hand sanitizer by a large margin | Zhong & Liljenquist [@zhong2006] |
| Cleanliness → moral judgment | d large | Being in a clean room makes you judge moral transgressions more harshly | Schnall, Haidt, Clore & Jordan [@schnall2008] |
| Flag exposure → political shift | d reported large | Glimpsing a US flag shifts political attitudes rightward for 8+ months | Carter, Ferguson & Hassin [@carter2011] |
| Heavy clipboard → weighty judgment | substantial | Holding a heavier clipboard makes you rate a CV as more important | Ackerman, Nocera & Bargh [@ackerman2010] |
| Disgust primes → moral condemnation | d large | A bad smell in the room makes you condemn unrelated moral transgressions more harshly | Schnall, Haidt, Clore & Jordan [@schnall2008] |
| Hand washing → reduces cognitive dissonance | substantial | Washing your hands after a choice makes you less likely to rationalize it | Lee & Schwarz [@lee2010] |
| Fast-food cues → impatience | substantial | Subliminally seeing a fast-food logo makes you less willing to save money | Zhong & DeVoe [@zhong2010] |
| Red → attractiveness judgments | d moderate-large | Wearing red (or having your photo on a red background) substantially raises your perceived attractiveness | Elliot & Niesta [@elliot2008] |
:::
